select_when: A high-stakes decision requires explicit downside sizing, scenario comparison, or sensitivity analysis rather than intuition alone.
The uncertainty parameters are estimable enough that cost-benefit logic, decision trees, or reference classes can improve the choice.
The quality of the decision process matters more than trying to guarantee a good outcome in an uncertain environment.
The work needs a structured way to compare bets, losses, and reversibility before committing capital, time, or reputation.
avoid_when: The team is treating calculable models as a substitute for judgment in a setting where the core drivers are still too unknown or unstable.
Quantification is becoming decorative certainty and is not changing the actual choice, hedge, or exposure limit.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the model focused on decisions-as-bets, downside sizing, and structured bet comparison rather than generic bravery or risk appetite rhetoric.
donor_drops: Did not preserve broad finance or strategy tooling sprawl because the raw source is strongest on scenario analysis, sensitivity, and explicit bet framing.
open_questions: The source spans advisory, strategy, and competitive settings, so the output label is intentionally broad but still decision-linked.

select_when.note: The strengths and playbook sections explicitly support decision trees, scenario analysis, estimable uncertainty, and process-quality framing.

avoid_when.note: The source warns against false certainty, wrong framing, and treating calculations as more complete than the real uncertainty supports.

input_type.note: The source activates around high-stakes risk tradeoffs, while the compact type label is reviewed normalization.

output_type.note: The playbook yields explicit, compared bets and downside-aware decisions; the output label is a compact reviewed abstraction.

reasoning_types.note: The source is probabilistic in how it sizes bets and causal in how it reasons about drivers and consequences.
